---
title: "Dependency Exclusions"
tagline: "What to Do When Excluding a Dependency Fixes the Build but Breaks Runtime"
type: scenario
tool: maven
---

#### ! Scenario What to Do When Excluding a Dependency Fixes the Build but Breaks Runtime


This scenario demonstrates how to surgically remove a transitive dependency from the graph.

This is a common "sledgehammer" fix for conflicts.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.0.0]
    LibA -.-> LibC[lib-c:1.0.0]
    Root -- excludes --> LibC
```


```{.xml include="pom.xml" snippet="s5-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Excluding `lib-c` removes it and its transitive subtree from the graph entirely.

#### ! Why
Maven respects exclusion metadata and prunes the specified branch during graph traversal. This "sledgehammer" fix for conflicts is why the same graph behaves differently across tools.

#### ! Fix
This *is* the fix for many problems (e.g., excluding `log4j` to use `slf4j`).

#### ! Risk
* LOW Replacing an implementation (e.g., `log4j` with `logback` + bridge).
* MEDIUM Excluding a transitive you believe is unused; future versions may require it.
* HIGH Excluding to "fix" a conflict without understanding the dependency; this frequently causes `NoClassDefFoundError` at runtime.

#### ! Check

**Fast check:** `mvn dependency:tree`

**What to look for:** Absence of the dependency under the declaring artifact. If it remains elsewhere, it is being pulled in through another path.

**Conclusion:** The exclusion successfully prunes the dependency from this path.

#### ! Scales
Overuse of exclusions leads to "Swiss cheese" dependency graphs that are hard to reason about.

#### ! Takeaway
Exclusions are powerful but dangerous; use them only when you know the dependent library can function without the excluded artifact (or you are providing a replacement).

#### ! Related
*   [MS15](../scenario-15-substitution/guide.qmd) - Replacing a library instead of just removing it.

#### ! Wild

For years, the most common use of exclusions in the Java ecosystem was logging. Prior to Spring Framework 5, any project that wanted to use SLF4J had to explicitly exclude `commons-logging` from Spring's transitive dependencies -- a pattern so widespread that Spring's own [blog documented the problem](https://spring.io/blog/2009/12/04/logging-dependencies-in-spring/) and the [SLF4J FAQ](https://www.slf4j.org/faq.html) devoted a section to it. Spring eventually solved it in 5.0 by replacing `commons-logging` with its own [`spring-jcl` bridge module](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/core/spring-jcl.html). The logging exclusion pattern remains common in projects that consolidate multiple logging frameworks (e.g., excluding `log4j` in favour of `logback`, or removing `slf4j-simple` to prevent binding conflicts).

License compliance scans do frequently uncover GPL-licensed libraries pulled in transitively. However, the fix is rarely as simple as excluding and substituting -- permissively licensed drop-in replacements with compatible APIs are uncommon. More often, teams must get legal to approve the transitive inclusion, restructure the code to avoid the dependency entirely, or accept the license obligation.
